explicit_text: I was born and raised in Waurika, Oklahoma, a small town in the Midwest. Growing up on a farm there with a large family taught me the values of hard work, conservatism, and sharing. My parents were hardworking farmers, and life on the farm involved growing vegetables, cotton, corn, and raising livestock. Those early years in Waurika shaped much of who I became.

Those early years in that small Oklahoma community shaped much of who I became.

explicit_text: My brother served in the United States Marine Corps during World War II, which was a point of family pride. Although I did not serve in the Marines myself, his service was part of the military background in my family. It was after my college days and draft that I entered military service, but in the Army rather than the Marines.

Although I did not serve in that branch myself, his service was part of the military background in my family. It was after my college days and draft that I entered military service, but in the Army rather than the Marines.

explicit_text: I entered the military in 1956 and 1957, which was the post-Korean War period. Although I did not serve during the Korean War itself, the timing of my enlistment was shortly after that conflict. The military environment I joined was shaped by the aftermath of the Korean War.

explicit_text: After serving in Germany, I was transferred back to Fort Benning, Georgia, to join an infantry unit. However, I found that even infantry at Fort Benning was not active enough for me, which led me to pursue Special Forces training. Fort Benning was a key place in my military career development.
implicit_text: After serving in Germany, I was transferred back to a major infantry training base in Georgia to join an infantry unit. However, I found that even infantry at that post was not active enough for me, which led me to pursue Special Forces training. That Georgia installation was a key place in my military career development.

explicit_text: In 1961, I applied for and was accepted into the United States Army Special Forces, which I considered the most elite unit in the military. After about a year and a half of rigorous training, I qualified fully and was proud to wear the green beret. Special Forces training prepared me for missions in places like Laos and Vietnam, and it was a defining part of my military career.
implicit_text: In 1961, I applied for and was accepted into the elite special operations unit of the U.S. Army, which I considered the most elite unit in the military. After about a year and a half of rigorous training, I qualified fully and was proud to wear the distinctive headgear. This specialized training prepared me for missions in places like Laos and Vietnam, and it was a defining part of my military career.

explicit_text: The green beret was a prestigious symbol authorized by President John F. Kennedy for the Army Special Forces. I was honored to be selected as the honor guard at the ceremony that officially authorized the green beret. Wearing it represented my qualification and pride in being part of the Special Forces.

explicit_text: Initially, Special Forces training was oriented toward the Middle East, which was part of the global strategic focus at the time. However, my own assignments shifted toward Southeast Asia, specifically Laos and Vietnam. The Middle East was the original training focus before my deployment orders changed.

However, my own assignments shifted toward Southeast Asia, specifically Laos and Vietnam. That region was the original training focus before my deployment orders changed.

explicit_text: I was initially training to be assigned to Laos with the Special Forces, but at the last minute, my assignment was changed to Vietnam. Laos was part of the early mission planning in the region, and the Special Forces had operations there. My experience in Laos was more indirect, as my actual deployments were focused in Vietnam.
implicit_text: I was initially training to be assigned to a landlocked Southeast Asian country with mountainous terrain and a communist insurgency, but at the last minute, my assignment was changed to a neighboring country to the east. That country was part of the early mission planning in the region, and the Special Forces had operations there. My experience in that landlocked nation was more indirect, as my actual deployments were focused in the neighboring country.

explicit_text: My first trip to Vietnam began in February 1963, where I operated in civilian clothes as part of the Civilian Irregular Defense Group. I worked closely with Vietnamese Special Forces, training local civilian forces to protect their villages. Vietnam was a complex and challenging environment, culturally and militarily, and I served multiple tours there, including in the A Shau Valley.
implicit_text: My first trip to that Southeast Asian country began in February 1963, where I operated in civilian clothes as part of a program to train irregular local defense forces. I worked closely with indigenous special operations forces, training local civilian groups to protect their villages. That country was a complex and challenging environment, culturally and militarily, and I served multiple tours there, including in a strategic valley near the Laotian border.

explicit_text: In Vietnam, I was part of the Civilian Irregular Defense Group, which involved advising Vietnamese Special Forces and training civilian forces to defend their own villages. Our mission was to train groups and then move on to new areas to repeat the process. This was a key part of the counterinsurgency efforts during my early Vietnam service.

explicit_text: The A Shau Valley was a jungle, mountainous region in northern South Vietnam near the Laotian border where I was assigned during my second tour. It was a strategically important area, located in the middle of the Ho Chi Minh trail and heavily contested. Our Special Forces team, A102, was stationed there, and the valley was the site of intense combat and infiltration by North Vietnamese forces.
implicit_text: The valley was a jungle, mountainous region in northern South Vietnam near the Laotian border where I was assigned during my second tour. It was a strategically important area, located in the middle of a major North Vietnamese supply route and heavily contested. Our Special Forces team, designated A102, was stationed there, and the valley was the site of intense combat and infiltration by enemy forces.

explicit_text: The Ho Chi Minh trail ran right through the A Shau Valley, making it a critical supply and infiltration route for the North Vietnamese Army. Our camp in the valley was constantly under threat because the trail was bombed daily but quickly repaired. Monitoring and disrupting this trail was a major part of our mission in Vietnam.
implicit_text: The major supply and infiltration route for the North Vietnamese Army ran right through that contested valley, making it a critical artery for their operations. Our camp in the valley was constantly under threat because the trail was bombed daily but quickly repaired. Monitoring and disrupting this route was a major part of our mission in the war.

explicit_text: The North Vietnamese Army was the primary enemy force we faced in the A Shau Valley and throughout Vietnam. We had intelligence from defectors that a large division, the 325th Infantry, was preparing to attack our camp. They launched heavy mortar and RPG attacks against us, making our position extremely dangerous and contested.
implicit_text: The primary enemy force we faced in that contested valley and throughout the war was the communist army from North Vietnam. We had intelligence from defectors that a large division was preparing to attack our camp. They launched heavy mortar and RPG attacks against us, making our position extremely dangerous and contested.

explicit_text: The Viet Cong were one of the enemy forces operating in the region, controlling roads and jungle areas around our camp in the A Shau Valley. They worked alongside the North Vietnamese Army to challenge U.S. and South Vietnamese forces. Their presence made ground movement impossible, forcing us to rely on air access.
implicit_text: The communist guerrilla forces were one of the enemy groups operating in the region, controlling roads and jungle areas around our camp in the contested valley. They worked alongside the North Vietnamese Army to challenge U.S. and South Vietnamese forces. Their presence made ground movement impossible, forcing us to rely on air access.

explicit_text: The 325th Infantry Division was the North Vietnamese unit identified by defectors as preparing to attack our Special Forces camp in the A Shau Valley. They launched a large-scale assault on March 9, 1966, involving heavy mortar and artillery fire. This division was a formidable enemy force during my second tour in Vietnam.
implicit_text: The infantry division identified by defectors as preparing to attack our Special Forces camp in the contested valley was a formidable enemy unit. They launched a large-scale assault on March 9, 1966, involving heavy mortar and artillery fire. This division was a formidable enemy force during my second tour in that war.

explicit_text: On March 9, 1966, our Special Forces camp in the A Shau Valley came under a full-scale attack by the North Vietnamese Army. The battle began with heavy mortar fire from 82-mm and 120-mm mortars and RPG attacks, followed by mass infantry assaults. It was one of the most intense engagements I experienced during my service in Vietnam.

explicit_text: Saigon was the location of the jails from which some of the indigenous soldiers guarding our camp were taken as punishment. It was the political and military center of South Vietnam during the war. While I did not serve directly in Saigon, it was part of the broader context of the conflict and the forces we were working with.
